Areas of expertise
⚡

Hydrogen & Fuel Cell Systems

Development, full-system commissioning and maritime certification of a 500 kW modular PEM fuel cell and steam reformer — marine, bus, and truck applications.

At Freudenberg e-Power Systems, I was responsible for the development and commissioning of a 500 kW modular fuel cell system. My role covered the complete lifecycle — from requirements specification through supplier management, system integration, commissioning, and certification.

I managed approximately 20 international suppliers with strategic focus on the DACH region (Germany, Austria, Switzerland) to ensure rapid engineering response, strong communication, and effective relationship management throughout the supply chain.

I was personally responsible for the commissioning of the complete 500 kW system, including cold, warm, and hot phases. I specified individual commissioning steps based on system requirements and applicable safety regulations.

Steam reformer — maritime: In parallel, I contributed to a maritime steam reformer project — converting liquid hydrocarbons into hydrogen-rich gas for shipboard fuel cell operation. All systems certified under Lloyd's Register and ABS.

PEM fuel cell principle — H₂ oxidized at anode, O₂ reduced at cathode → electricity, water, heat. Zero direct CO₂ emissions.
High-power fuel cell systems enable zero-emission marine vessels, buses, and heavy-duty trucks.
500 kW PEM fuel cellSteam reformer (maritime)Full system commissioning ~20 international suppliersDACH supplier focusComponent ownership Deputy team leadPolarion ALM/RMFMEA Lloyd's RegisterABSIATF 16949IEC 61000 Marine · Bus · Truck
⚙️

Compressors, Pumps, Valves & Injection Systems

Industrial component ownership of fluid machinery plus deep research into high-pressure GDI injectors via synchrotron X-ray and optical diagnostics.

At Freudenberg, I owned all critical fluid and mechanical components in the 500 kW fuel cell system: turbo compressors, centrifugal and diaphragm pumps, on/off and safety relief valves, water separators, vent valves, process filters, and control valves. Leak and pressure tests were performed per Lloyd's Register/ABS requirements; commissioning included cold, warm, and hot phases.

GDI injection research at Bosch: Injectors operate at up to 200 bar with multi-phase flow phenomena at micrometer scales. Using transparent nozzles, high-speed shadowgraphy, micro-PIV (5 MHz), and synchrotron XPCI at Argonne National Laboratory, I produced the first experimental data linking transient needle dynamics directly to near-nozzle spray structure.

GDI nozzle layouts Type A & B (Mamaikin et al., IJER) — aluminum nozzles for X-ray transparency, two inclination angles, hole diameter ~0.175–0.185 mm, injection up to 200 bar.
Cavitation probability maps — cylindrical vs. divergent holes, injection into air vs. liquid (Mamaikin et al., ILASS 2017, 120,000 fps).

📖 Publication figure browser

Cavitation at 1–7 bar backpressure — cylindrical holes. Higher backpressure increases fluctuation frequency and reduces average cavitation extent.
Dominant cavitation fluctuation frequency vs. backpressure. Divergent holes produce more stable, lower-frequency cavitation structures.

Mamaikin et al., ILASS–Europe 2017, Valencia — real-scale two-hole GDI nozzles, Cavilux HF laser, 120,000 fps, FFT analysis.

Graphic abstract — micro-PIV velocity field (left) and RANS-reconstructed static pressure (right) inside real-scale GDI nozzle at 100 bar. First experimental pressure data of this kind.
String cavitation initiation at 5 MHz (Δt = 0.2 μs). Two origins — lower hole and near-needle — merge into a continuous string extending to hole exit.

Mamaikin et al., Experiments in Fluids 61:154, 2020, Springer Nature — ultra-high-speed micro-PIV and RANS pressure reconstruction.

Synchrotron XPCI image sequence — internal cavitation (highlighted) and near-nozzle jet structure simultaneously visible. Hydraulic flip during needle overshoot identified.
Near-nozzle jet images from Type B nozzle during overshoot. Smooth and perturbed surface domains reveal transient in-nozzle cavitation state.

Mamaikin et al., IJER — synchrotron XPCI at 67,889 fps, Argonne National Laboratory, Advanced Photon Source 7ID beamline.

🔬 Interactive: backpressure effect on cavitation frequency

At 4 bar: cylindrical ~20–30 kHz · divergent ~12–18 kHz · cavitation film thins → fluctuation area increases

🌊

Fluid Mechanics

From nuclear boiling regimes to GDI spray cavitation — deep experimental and numerical expertise in complex two-phase and turbulent flows.

My fluid mechanics expertise spans the full range — from boiling physics (nucleate, transition, film boiling, Leidenfrost effect on superheated surfaces in nuclear power plant safety context) through combustion spray (laminar burning velocities with Heat Flux burner; LDA/PDA droplet measurements) to cavitating high-pressure injector flows.

A key methodological achievement of my PhD was combining 2D micro-PIV velocity data with RANS-based pressure reconstruction to yield the first experimental static pressure distribution inside a real-scale GDI nozzle. The fluctuating RANS terms were used to identify rotating flow structures and locate cavitation-prone regions — enabling 3D characterization from 2D data.

Innovation (Exp. Fluids 2020): RANS fluctuation analysis of PIV sequences predicts string cavitation onset locations without direct cavitation visualization — unique approach to 3D flow characterization from 2D measurements.

Mean velocity field (micro-PIV, 5 MHz) and RANS pressure distribution inside GDI nozzle at 100 bar. Velocity 10–130 m/s; pressure 2–100 bar. (Exp. Fluids 2020)
Static pressure (left) and RANS fluctuating term contribution (right) — two fluctuation regions predict string cavitation locations. (Exp. Fluids 2020)

🎬 Injection phase explorer — what happens inside the nozzle?

XPCI during needle overshoot — cavitation on both hole sides (hydraulic flip). Narrow, smooth jet with low divergence. (IJER, Argonne)

Hydraulic flip: During the transient opening, the needle reaches ~2× its steady-state lift. Near-symmetric inflow from both sac and needle-seat sides → cavitation forms on both hole walls simultaneously. The fuel jet passes without wall contact → narrow, smooth, low-divergence spray. First observed in aluminum GDI nozzle via synchrotron XPCI at Argonne.

Steady-state pressure field (RANS from micro-PIV). Minimum pressure on inner hole side — primary cavitation site. (Exp. Fluids 2020)

Steady-state injection: Flow arrives predominantly from the sac. A strong pressure drop at the inner hole inlet drives partial supercavitation. Asymmetric velocity profile: max velocity near outer side, recirculation on inner side. RANS fluctuating terms reveal two instability zones matching observed string cavitation locations. Measured velocity: 10–130 m/s at 100 bar injection pressure.

String cavitation initiation during closing at 5 MHz (Δt = 0.2 μs). Two origins merge into a continuous structure — from lower hole and near-needle. (Exp. Fluids 2020)

Needle closing: Descending needle dramatically increases sac vorticity, promoting string cavitation — vortex-core gas threads. Two separate origins merge into a single structure extending needle-to-exit. Captured at 5 million frames/second with Kirana camera + SILUX400 laser (10 ns pulses).

Experimental velocity (micro-PIV) vs. CFD (ANSYS CFX) comparison — good agreement in bulk flow; differences near inner side explained by test fuel properties. (Exp. Fluids 2020)
Quantitative transient jet parameters over injection time — boundary angles, width, direction, expansion angle. Clear transitions at overshoot / steady-state boundary. (IJER)
Backpressure effect on divergent holes — more robust cavitation structures vs. cylindrical holes. Average distribution barely changes with increasing backpressure. (ILASS 2017)
Two-phase flowCavitation (geometric · string · hydraulic flip) Spray dynamics & primary breakupRANS / URANS Pressure reconstruction (Poisson)ANSYS CFX Heat transferBoiling / Leidenfrost effect Laminar burning velocityLDA / PDA EV battery thermal management
🔬

Optics, Laser & Advanced Measurement Technology

High-speed optical diagnostics from laboratory shadowgraphy to synchrotron X-ray phase-contrast imaging at Argonne National Laboratory.

XPCI experimental setup at Argonne National Laboratory 7ID beamline (Mamaikin et al., IJER). Synchrotron radiation at 67,889 fps, 150 ps X-ray pulses, spatial resolution 2.53 μm/px.
Needle lift tracking via XPCI image processing — needle motion quantified from contrast changes in X-ray images. Overshoot to 2× steady-state lift clearly resolved. (IJER)

My optical diagnostics expertise covers the full range from conventional high-speed imaging to state-of-the-art synchrotron radiation techniques. Core methods include:

High-speed shadowgraphy — Cavilux HF laser (810 nm, 50 ns pulses), 120,000 fps at 640×216 px, long-distance microscope (Infinity K2, CF4 lens), 7.4× magnification. Applied for real-scale GDI nozzle cavitation at 200 bar.

Micro-PIV at 5 MHz — ultra-high-speed Kirana camera + SILUX400 laser (10 ns pulses), 180 frames per burst, 1.9 μm/px spatial resolution. Novel backlight illumination approach for PIV inside opaque-walled nozzles. Graphite tracer particles (3.5 μm).

Synchrotron XPCI at Argonne (APS 7ID) — X-ray phase-contrast imaging using synchrotron radiation. Hybrid-singlet mode, 150 ps single bunch illumination, 2.53 μm/px, Photron SA-Z camera. Simultaneous visualization of in-nozzle flow and near-nozzle spray in metal/aluminum nozzles.

LDA/PDA (MSc, Moscow): Optical droplet investigation using Laser Doppler Anemometry and Phase Doppler Anemometry — simultaneous size and velocity distributions from spray systems.

Transient jet characteristics: low vs. high inclination holes. High inclination hole less affected by needle overshoot due to geometric guidance of dominant sac-side flow. (IJER)
FFT spectral amplitude of projected cavitation area — dominant frequencies identified after Tukey-windowed third-order polynomial detrending. Sampling rate: 60 kHz (Nyquist limit). (ILASS 2017)
Micro-PIV (5 MHz)Synchrotron XPCI (Argonne APS) High-speed shadowgraphy (120,000 fps)LDA / PDA Heat Flux burnerLong-distance microscopy Image processing (FFT, binarization, cross-correlation) LabVIEW · IMC FamosMATLAB · Python
🤖

Machine Learning & Data Science

Active certification program building on a strong foundation in scientific data analysis, image processing, and experimental physics.

Throughout my research career I processed large experimental datasets — PIV velocity fields with millions of vectors, time-resolved image sequences at up to 5 MHz, multi-channel fuel cell sensor data, and FFT-analyzed cavitation signals. All analysis performed in MATLAB and Python.

Engineering data science methods applied: PIV cross-correlation, FFT spectral analysis, RANS statistical decomposition, iterative Poisson pressure integration (successive over-relaxation), image binarization and entropy filtering (XPCI), vortex model fitting (Gaussian/Lamb-Oseen).
